content.full_text:
RAy! MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
August 5, 1997
Jeffrey Sabatini
330 North Sixth Street, Suite 106
Redlands, California 92373
Re: Your Request for Advice
V
Our File No. 1-97-318
Dear Mr. Sabatini:
Thank you for your letter of June 6, 1997, which “follows-up” your previous letter to this
office ofMarch 31, 1997.
Our response to your letter of March 31, 1997, is contained in our
Advice Letter, 1-97-189; a copy of that Advice Letter is enclosed for your convenience.
In your June 6, 1997, letter you ask us to revisit our prior advice to you in light of the fact
that you have now decided not to run for office in the upcoming election. Because your question
is not based on specific, contemplated action, we will provide you with informal assistance only.
As you may be aware, informal assistance does not provide immunity for any action you may
take in reliance on the assistance. (2 Cal. Code ofRegulations, section 18329, copy enclosed.)
Your present question asks ifthe Act,
1 and specifically Proposition 208, regulates your
ability to publicize political messages ifyou are not currently a candidate and have no intention
to run for political office in the upcoming election.
Our answer does not change from that given in our previous letter to you. Neither the Act
nor Proposition 208 regulate the expenditure ofpersonal funds by a non-candidate or non-
officeholder exercising his or her right of free speech. Once a person makes an expenditure with
a view to bringing about his or her nomination or election to any elective office, he or she
becomes a candidate for office, and the Act and Proposition 208 will regulate that person’s
conduct. The legal analysis for this conclusion is contained in our letter, 1-97-189.
Government Code sections 81000 -91014.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-3 18
Page 2
In conclusion, ifyou are not a candidate and have no intent to become one at the time you
place political satire in a newspaper, you are not governed by the requirements ofthe Act and
Proposition 208 even though your name is indicated on the political satire.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Lisa L. Ditora
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:LLD:tls

sections.question: Does the Act, specifically Proposition 208, regulate the ability to publicize political messages if the person is not currently a candidate and has no intention to run for political office in the upcoming election?
sections.conclusion: If you are not a candidate and have no intent to become one at the time you place political satire in a newspaper, you are not governed by the requirements of the Act and Proposition 208 even though your name is indicated on the political satire.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether California's Political Reform Act and Proposition 208 regulate his ability to publicize political messages when he is not a candidate and has no intention to run for office.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Non-candidates and non-officeholders exercising free speech rights are not regulated by the Political Reform Act or Proposition 208, even if their names appear on political messages, unless they make expenditures with a view to their own nomination or election.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter; QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections clearly present in document; document references prior advice letter 1-97-189; notes that informal assistance does not provide immunity

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act, specifically Proposition 208, regulate the ability to publicize political messages if the person is not currently a candidate and has no intention to run for political office in the upcoming election?

CONCLUSION: If you are not a candidate and have no intent to become one at the time you place political satire in a newspaper, you are not governed by the requirements of the Act and Proposition 208 even though your name is indicated on the political satire.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that the Political Reform Act and Proposition 208 do not regulate political messages by non-candidates who have no intention to run for office, even if their names appear on the materials, reaffirming its prior advice in letter 1-97-189.
